cases.title: Isolated Femur Fracture in a Stable Patient
cases.presentation: A 20-year-old soccer player has an isolated, painful, deformed mid-thigh. Distal pulses, motor, and sensation are intact. He is otherwise stable with normal vitals, and transport time is 25 minutes.
cases.question: What is the appropriate management of the leg?
options: Apply a traction splint after confirming no hip/knee/pelvis involvement, rechecking PMS before and after
Defer all splinting and do nothing
cases.explanation: An isolated mid-shaft femur fracture in a stable patient is the ideal indication for a traction splint. Confirm the hip, knee, and pelvis are not involved, assess distal PMS before and after, and apply only enough traction to realign and relieve pain.
cases.clinicalPearl: Traction splints shine for the isolated mid-shaft femur fracture—but only after you've excluded hip, knee, and pelvic involvement.

cases.title: Wrong Splint for the Injury
cases.presentation: A patient has an obviously deformed, painful knee after a fall. A trainee reaches for a traction splint to 'pull it straight.'
cases.question: Why is the traction splint the wrong choice here?
options: Traction splints are only for arms
Traction across a fractured/dislocated knee can tear the popliteal vessels—immobilize the knee in position instead
cases.explanation: Traction splints are only for mid-shaft femur fractures. Applying traction to a knee injury can lacerate the popliteal artery. Immobilize the knee in the position found (if pulses are intact) and reassess distal PMS.
cases.clinicalPearl: Never apply traction across the knee—splint joint injuries in the position found when perfusion is intact.

cases.title: Massive Extremity Hemorrhage
cases.presentation: A worker's arm was caught in machinery, leaving a deep, spurting wound at the upper arm. Direct pressure has not controlled the bleeding and he is becoming pale.
cases.question: What is the correct sequence of hemorrhage control?
options: Apply a tourniquet 2–3 inches proximal to the wound, tighten until bleeding stops and pulse is gone, note the time
Periodically loosen pressure to check the wound
cases.explanation: Arterial extremity hemorrhage uncontrolled by direct pressure requires a tourniquet placed 2–3 inches proximal to the wound (not over a joint), tightened until bleeding stops and the distal pulse disappears, with the time recorded. If a tourniquet cannot control a junctional component, pack with a hemostatic dressing.
cases.clinicalPearl: A tourniquet works only when tight enough to abolish the distal pulse—an under-tightened tourniquet bleeds more than none at all.
